images.caption: Figure 1:Motivating examples for hybrid GDN initialization.Left:zero-shot PPL is highly sensitive to how the newly introduced recurrent parameters are initialized, even when all variants reuse the same teacherQ/K/V/OQ/K/V/Oprojections.Right:in an actual Qwen3-8B training run, Taylor-Calibrate starts from a lower loss and maintains a better optimization trajectory than random initialization.

Figure 2:Overview of Taylor-Calibrate. Standard transfer copies teacher projections but leaves new GDN dynamics at default initialization. Taylor-Calibrate instead uses teacher attention statistics to calibrate recurrent gates, then performs a short per-layer alignment step before downstream distillation.

Figure 3:Recovery dynamics under matched distillation training for the main teacher settings. Each subfigure shows the current training dashboard, summarizing the available recovery trajectories across configurations.
Figure 4:RULER trajectory for the 1.5B setting during extended SFTMix v0.2 training.

Figure 5:Token-budget comparison at matched target quality. For each representative run, panel (a) reports the Stage-2 training tokens required for Baseline and Taylor-Calibrate to reach the same target performance, and panel (b) reports the resulting speedup factor. Across these runs, Taylor-Calibrate reduces the required budget by 4.9×\times–9.2×\times.
Figure 6:Recovery dynamics for the two Qwen2.5 teacher settings under matched distillation training.
Figure 7:Per-layer idle half-life statistics across recurrent GDN layers. Taylor calibration removes the heavy-tailed random decay outliers and produces a consistent memory timescale across layers.

Figure 8:Layer-wise magnitudes of the main GDN gate parameters. The Taylor-Only setting suppresses unstable random gate scales, while full Taylor-Calibrate restores non-trivial projection-gate magnitude after local alignment.

Figure 9:Parameter changes from Taylor-Only to full Taylor-Calibrate after layer-local alignment. The recurrent half-life remains nearly fixed, while the write and output projection gates receive most of the alignment-induced change.
